lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 9 - Attention, Final Projects and LLM Intro

unit.name: Baselines, Milestones, Reports, and Project Idea Patterns
unit.description: Explains how projects are evaluated, why baselines matter, what the milestone should demonstrate, and the main families of successful project ideas with concrete examples.
unit.summary: The lecturer emphasizes that every project needs an appropriate baseline; without a clear comparison point, it is impossible to tell whether a new method is actually good [ts=3494s] [ts=3554s]. He says the milestone should show that the team has data, code, and at least one runnable system that produces a measurable result, even if it is only a simple baseline [ts=3558s] [ts=3598s]. Final projects are judged largely through the report, which should read like a conference paper with related work, method, data, experiments, analysis, and conclusions [ts=3602s] [ts=3654s]. The segment then catalogs viable project types: task-driven systems, method-driven neural-network modifications, LLM-based system building, interpretability or behavior analysis, and occasional theoretical projects, illustrated with examples like deep poetry, differentiable neural-computer reimplementation, weight tying, quantized models, code-generation for Fortran, and image-to-text fashion cataloging [ts=3690s] [ts=4155s].

key_points.text: Every project should include a baseline that provides a meaningful comparison point.
A milestone should show that the team has a working setup that can run and produce a number.
The final report should be written like a conference paper with method, data, experiments, and analysis.
Successful custom projects can be task-driven, method-driven, interpretability-focused, or theoretical.
Reimplementing an interesting prior paper can be a valid project if it produces meaningful results.
Recent projects increasingly use pretrained LLMs with PEFT or in-context learning rather than training from scratch.

Final projects are judged largely through the report, which should read like a conference paper with related work, method, data, experiments, analysis, and conclusions [ts=3602s] [ts=3654s]. kp_catalog.name: Value add, baselines, and evaluation design
kp_catalog.description: Projects are judged by what new insight, comparison, or improvement they contribute, so they need baselines, measurable evaluation, and analysis rather than just model usage.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because value add, baselines, and evaluation design formalizes how projects are judged by what new insight, comparison, or improvement they contribute, so they need baselines, measurable evaluation, and analysis rather than just model usage, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Families of viable project types
kp_catalog.description: CS224N projects can be task-driven, method-driven, interpretability-oriented, theoretical, or reimplementation-based, provided they remain substantive and evaluable.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because families of viable project types clarifies cS224N projects can be task-driven, method-driven, interpretability-oriented, theoretical, or reimplementation-based, provided they remain substantive and evaluable, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: LLM-era project strategy
kp_catalog.description: Modern course projects often build on pretrained LLMs using PEFT, in-context learning, or API-driven experimentation, but still need a genuine research question and analysis.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because lLM-era project strategy clarifies modern course projects often build on pretrained LLMs using PEFT, in-context learning, or API-driven experimentation, but still need a genuine research question and analysis, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
